page: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-4xCPU-48GB is a memory-optimized virtual server running on the KVM hypervisor. It features 4 shared AMD EPYC 7542 vCPUs based on the x86_64 architecture, operating at a base clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 48.0 GB of system memory, providing a high resource density of 12.0 GB of RAM per core. No local storage or dedicated GPU hardware is bundled with this server type, and it includes zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Performance benchmarks indicate a clear tradeoff between compute power and memory capacity. The shared CPU allocation results in lower compute performance, with stress-ng single-core and multi-core benchmarks scoring in the poor and weak categories respectively. However, memory subsystem performance is more balanced; small-block read-only memory bandwidth reaches 411.75 GB/sec, and large-block write bandwidth is measured at 25.08 GB/sec. Text processing and single-threaded compression tasks perform at average levels, while database operations and static web serving throughput are relatively weak.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this server offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio without the premium cost of dedicated CPU cores, making it a viable option for memory-intensive workloads that are not CPU-bound. Appropriate workloads include caching layers, Redis deployments, lightweight text processing, and small-scale LLM inference tasks using models under 135M parameters. It is less suitable for heavy compilation, ray tracing, or large-scale database operations due to the shared CPU constraints.
description: The UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-4xCPU-48GB is an x86_64 virtual server featuring 4 shared AMD EPYC 7542 vCPUs running at 2.0 GHz and 48.0 GB of RAM. Operating on the KVM hypervisor, this server offers a high memory-to-CPU ratio of 12.0 GB per core, though it lacks local storage and GPU accelerators. Benchmark data reveals a performance profile optimized for memory capacity over raw compute power; while CPU-intensive tasks like multi-core stress tests and software compilation score in the lower percentiles, memory-centric operations such as small-block cached reads and Redis SET operations perform at average levels. Consequently, this server is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-heavy, burstable workloads. It is best suited for caching layers, text processing, and lightweight database or web-serving tasks that do not require sustained, dedicated CPU performance.
